Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

C. Lloyd Morgan

1852 – 1936

Emergence, animal mind, and graded continuity between life and consciousness.

C. Lloyd Morgan’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as organismic dual-aspect emergentism with a theistic-spiritual completion. He is not a reductive materialist, not a Cartesian dualist, not a simple behaviorist, and not straightforwardly a panpsychist. His core position is that organism and mind, body-story and mind-story, physiological process and conscious or mental process, are distinguishable but not separable aspects of one natural reality. Consciousness emerges in graded forms within organic evolution; mind is embodied, developmental, affective, intentional, and finally capable of reflective and religious reference. “Soul” is acceptable only when stripped of animistic dualism: it is not an independent entity lodged in the body, but a reflective-spiritual level of mind. Theology is not accidental to the system. Morgan’s Gifford philosophy insists that natural evolution and Divine Purpose belong to one order, not two rival explanations. God is not introduced as an occasional gap-cause, but as the ultimate spiritual ground or substance of the whole natural process.

A compact formula would be this: Morgan wants the mind to be real without being a ghost, natural without being reducible to mechanism, causally significant without violating psychophysical parallelism, and spiritually interpretable without invoking supernatural interruptions.

⸻

1. The textual map: Morgan’s shifting but continuous vocabulary

Morgan’s philosophy of mind develops across several phases. In the comparative-psychology writings, especially An Introduction to Comparative Psychology, he begins from descriptive consciousness, animal mentality, and the methodological problem of interpreting nonhuman minds. In Animal Behaviour, he sharpens his account of animal consciousness, instinct, intelligence, and the famous methodological “Canon.” In the Gifford lectures, especially Emergent Evolution and Life, Mind, and Spirit, he expands the psychological doctrine into a general metaphysics of emergence, substance, naturalism, and Divine Purpose. The title page and preface of Emergent Evolution identify it as the first course of Gifford Lectures delivered at St Andrews in 1922, while Life, Mind, and Spirit presents the second course, delivered in 1923 and published in 1926.

A major interpretive warning is needed at the outset: Morgan does not always use “mind” in exactly the same sense. In his early comparative psychology, “mind” is often almost synonymous with the wave of consciousness: the empirical psychologist’s subject matter is conscious experience as it comes, changes, and passes. In Emergent Evolution, “mind” can mean an emergent quality at a higher evolutionary level, but also the psychical correlate of a physical or vital process. He explicitly distinguishes levels such as matter, life, and mind, while also entertaining psychical correlates beneath full consciousness. In Life, Mind, and Spirit, he deliberately drops some older terminology and uses “mind” broadly: every life-process has a mental concomitant, though not every such concomitant is reflective consciousness or even focal awareness.

This shifting vocabulary can make Morgan look inconsistent. A better reading is developmental. His project becomes progressively broader: from empirical consciousness, to animal mind, to emergence, to a spiritual metaphysics of nature. The continuity is supplied by four commitments: monism, dual-aspect analysis, emergence, and the refusal to treat mind as either a mechanical by-product or a supernatural intruder.

⸻

2. The early starting point: experience before the subject/object split

Morgan begins with experience. In An Introduction to Comparative Psychology, he argues that the relation of mind and organism cannot be avoided once psychology is treated evolutionarily. His preferred view is monistic: subject and object are distinguishable aspects within one indivisible experience, not two separate worlds. Mind is not “extranatural” or “supra-natural”; it is one aspect of natural existence. Body and mind are products of analysis, while the living organism is given as one and indivisible. Brain changes and consciousness are therefore not two substances externally conjoined, but two coordinated aspects of one event.

This is decisive for the whole system. Morgan is not beginning from a Cartesian problem of how an immaterial soul can influence a material body. He starts from the lived unity of the organism and then analyzes it into physical and mental aspects. The ordinary dualist starts with two things and asks how they interact. Morgan starts with one natural occurrence and asks how it may be read under two irreducible modes of analysis.

His phenomenology of consciousness is also processual. He refuses to define consciousness by reducing it to something else, because consciousness is the primary datum of direct experience. The present moment of consciousness is not a mathematical instant but a “wave”: it has a focal crest, dawning and waning slopes, and marginal or subconscious elements. The past and future enter consciousness as present representations. Conscious states arise, alter, and pass, but the wave has continuity, especially through its marginal body rather than through sharply defined focal contents.

This “wave” model matters because it already resists atomistic psychology. Mind is not a succession of isolated mental atoms. A present conscious state includes focus, margin, fringe, retention, anticipation, bodily tone, and subconscious background. Introspection itself is not pure immediate inspection but a form of retrospection: by the time one observes a mental process, it has already entered the past of consciousness.

Morgan’s later concepts of “enjoyment,” “reference,” and spiritual attitude are rooted in this early insight. Consciousness is always more than explicit representation. It has affective, bodily, marginal, and directional structure before it becomes reflective thought.

⸻

3. Brain, body, and the “one curve” doctrine

In the early physiological chapters, Morgan takes a careful line. Conscious states are psychical, not physical; direct acquaintance is with one’s own consciousness. Yet the reproduction of ideas depends on bodily and especially cerebral conditions, and the brain is treated as the seat or condition of consciousness. He rejects the simple notion that past ideas literally persist as psychical entities waiting to be retrieved. Instead, apparent “retention” depends on organic conditions enabling reproduction or revival.

He then contrasts empirical dualism with monism. The ordinary dualist view says that mind or soul uses the body and may be released at death. Morgan’s monism instead treats the physiological and the psychical as distinguishable but indivisible. He compares them to one curve viewed under two aspects. The point is not that the mental is really physical, nor that the physical is really mental, but that one concrete process admits two coordinate descriptions.

Morgan also introduces a speculative but important idea: infra-consciousness. Below the threshold of focal consciousness there may be processes that are not yet, or not quite, conscious. This is not unconsciousness as mere nothingness; it is a positive sub-threshold condition, though Morgan knows the term is philosophically hazardous. In his later thought, this becomes one route toward the idea that mind has primitive or attenuated forms before it reaches reflective awareness.

In his chapter on the evolution of consciousness, Morgan asks how consciousness can be placed within evolution. He rejects a simple materialist derivation in which consciousness abruptly comes from mere energy as something wholly unlike it. He also treats special creation sympathetically but does not adopt it as a scientific explanation. His preferred speculative line is that consciousness evolves from simpler infra-consciousness, while both energy and consciousness are grounded in a deeper underlying activity that is neither merely energy nor merely consciousness, neither subject nor object.

This is one of the most delicate points in Morgan. He does not say that full consciousness is everywhere. Nor does he reduce consciousness to physics. Nor does he make consciousness an inexplicable miracle. Rather, he proposes a graded metaphysics: conscious mind emerges out of infra-conscious or psychical correlates, while the whole natural order is grounded in an underlying activity deeper than the physical/psychical distinction. That underlying activity later receives a more explicitly theological interpretation.

⸻

4. Animal mind and Morgan’s Canon: methodological parsimony, not behaviorist denial

Morgan is best known for the Canon: animal behavior should not be interpreted as the outcome of a higher mental process if it can be adequately explained by a lower psychological process. In Animal Behaviour, he states the principle while making clear that it does not forbid higher interpretations when there is independent evidence for them. The Canon is therefore not a denial of animal consciousness. It is a rule against premature anthropomorphic explanation.

This point is often misunderstood. Morgan’s Canon is compatible with a robust animal psychology because Morgan thinks animals possess genuine perceptual consciousness, affective interest, practical meanings, and intelligent adjustment. What he resists is the projection of reflective, ideational, human-like reasoning into animals when perceptual or practical processes suffice. He argues that much animal behavior is perceptual rather than ideational: humans construct plans “in the head,” whereas a trained dog may participate in a human plan without originating such a plan reflectively.

Morgan distinguishes intelligent control and rational control. Intelligent control is perceptual and practical; rational control is ideational and reflective. Consciousness may accompany behavior, guide it, and judge it. He also distinguishes sentience, consentience, and consciousness, thereby giving animal mind a layered structure rather than a binary “conscious/unconscious” status.

His analysis of instinct is especially important. He rejects “impulse” as a metaphysical force and treats it instead as an unreflective conative tendency. Instinctive action occurs in a conscious situation, but the self/world distinction is not yet fully articulated. The same bodily-brain process generates a mental unity and lays the basis for later differentiation between self and world. At the perceptual stage there is not yet a continuous reflective self facing a continuous objective world; such ideal construction comes later.

This gives Morgan a developmental account of the self. The animal does not begin as a little Cartesian ego. The germ of selfhood is embodied in interested behavior. Things become centers of interest through practical dealings. Subject/object and self/world polarize late out of a more primitive organism-environment field. Emotions are also embodied: organic, visceral, and motor elements enter the conscious situation, and feeling-tone directs conative tendency.

Morgan also explicitly rejects the view that consciousness is a useless “steam-whistle” added to behavior. If consciousness had no role, its evolutionary presence would be difficult to understand. Yet he avoids saying that consciousness as a separate psychical force pushes physical molecules. Under monism, a molecular disturbance and a conscious state are the same occurrence under different aspects. To say that consciousness influences behavior is shorthand for saying that the neural situation, described under its psychological aspect, belongs to the guidance of behavior.

This is the mature pattern in miniature: mental efficacy without dualistic interaction.

⸻

5. Emergent evolution: novelty without supernatural insertion

In Emergent Evolution, Morgan generalizes his psychology into an ontology. Emergent evolution emphasizes the coming-in of the genuinely new: atoms, molecules, organisms, minds, reflective thought. These belong to one tissue of events and are to be treated scientifically, but their novelty must not be erased. Morgan wants neither reductionism nor a resort to special vital forces.

His famous distinction is between resultants and emergents. A resultant is additive or predictable from prior components; an emergent is a new quality or relational order that cannot be predicted from the lower-level terms before it appears. Yet emergence does not mean a break in natural causation. Resultants provide continuity; emergents provide progress and novelty. There is no hiatus in nature, but there is also no mere summation.

Morgan uses levels such as A, B, and C to mark matter, life, and mind. There is no mind without life, and no life without a physical basis. The higher level depends on the lower, but it is not merely the lower. Life is not a detachable vital force; mind is not a detachable psychic force. They are emergent qualities or systems of relatedness arising within more complex natural wholes.

This is where his anti-vitalism and anti-reductionism meet. He rejects “entelechy,” “élan,” or any alien influence inserted into matter from outside. The emergent quality is natural, but it is not predictable from lower-level inspection alone.

Morgan’s position on mental causation is subtle. He argues against radical behaviorism by insisting that mental guidance is effective: when the supervenient mental relatedness is present, the course of events differs. But this effectiveness is not a new force acting across an ontological gulf. It is the effectiveness of the whole organized situation at the level at which mind is present.

He also modernizes a broadly Spinozistic doctrine. Physical and psychical are attributes or aspects of the same evolutionary process; there is no causal relation between attributes. One and the same evolution is expressed in different ways. This doctrine lets him reject both materialism and dualist interactionism. The physical does not produce the mental as a separate effect, and the mental does not intervene in the physical as a foreign cause. They are concomitant expressions of one real process.

⸻

6. The mature doctrine of life-mind concomitance

In Life, Mind, and Spirit, Morgan modifies and expands his terminology. He accepts a thoroughgoing naturalism while also affirming belief in God. Naturalism, for him, includes the evolutionary development of bodily and mental life; life and mind are distinguished from spirit, yet life and mind are manifestations of spirit. Spirit is not merely the final emergent quality at the top of the pyramid, but that of which all qualities are manifestations.

He also develops a metaphysics of substance and stuff. “Stuff” refers to the items or constituents; “substance” refers to their specific going-together, the integral unity of an entity. An atom, molecule, or organism is not a mere heap; it has substantial unity insofar as its constituents are integrated into an indivisible whole.

This matters for mind because mind is not a second stuff added to living stuff. The organism is one substantial unity, analysable under physiological and psychological attributes. Morgan therefore replaces or supplements “correlation” with concomitance. An organism may be regarded physiologically as life and psychologically as mind; no life occurs without mind, and no mind occurs except in concomitance with life-processes.

Here he distinguishes broader and narrower senses of “conscious.” In the narrower sense, conscious action involves intention, attention, or guidance. In the broader sense, all vital action may be called conscious because it has mental concomitants or modes of feeling. This broader use can sound extravagant, but it is not a claim that every organism has human-like reflective awareness. It is a claim that living process and mental concomitance are inseparable at some level.

Morgan admits that direct evidence is limited. One has direct access only to one’s own mental events, and one infers other minds from behavior. The inference becomes increasingly speculative as one descends from humans to animals, to anemones, amoebae, or plants. He nevertheless adopts unrestricted concomitance as a working hypothesis: mental events accompany all living processes, even when no nervous system is present.

This is where the panpsychism question arises. Morgan is not a simple panpsychist if panpsychism means that all matter is conscious. He is closer to a biopsychical dual-aspect emergentist in Life, Mind, and Spirit: wherever there is life, there is mind in an attenuated sense; higher consciousness emerges through increasingly complex living organization. In Emergent Evolution, he also entertains psychical correlates at lower levels, but he is careful to mark the speculative status of such extension.

⸻

7. Enjoyment and reference: Morgan’s mature psychology of mind

Morgan’s most fruitful mature distinction is between enjoyment and reference. “Enjoyment” is not mere pleasure. It is the intrinsic felt side of living unity: the organism’s own inward mode of being, feeling, or undergoing. “Reference” is the outward, cognitive, object-directed, or prospective side of mind.

Physical influence and mental reference are radically different. A physical influence travels through causal channels; mental reference does not physically travel to its object. Morgan’s example of reference to Sirius is meant to show that when one thinks of Sirius, a mental reference is directed to Sirius, but no physical process goes from the mind to the star in the way a causal signal would. The life-story is a story of physical influence; the mind-story is a story of progressive reference.

This is a highly important philosophy-of-mind thesis. Morgan is trying to protect intentionality from reduction to physical contact. Mental reference is real, but it is not a physical relation of transmission. Reference emerges within embodied behavior and cognition, but its logical structure is different from bodily influence.

He then develops a hierarchy. At the highest level is reflective reference: rational thought, conceptual objectivity, and self-consciousness. Below that is cognitive but unreflective reference in intelligent animals and infants. Below that are more primitive forms of enjoyment and practical orientation.

Enjoyment is concomitant with life. Pleasure and pain are modes within enjoyment, but enjoyment is broader than hedonic tone. The organism’s physiological poise is also a poise of enjoyment and, where developed, of reference. There is no living process that is merely physiological in abstraction from its mental concomitant.

This gives Morgan a powerful account of consciousness as layered:

At the base is enjoyment, the intrinsic felt unity of life.

Above it is cognitive reference, the practical directedness toward objects in perception, action, and learning.

Above that is reflective reference, the explicit conceptual relation to self, world, truth, value, and purpose.

Above reflective reference, or emerging through it, is spiritual reference, the religious attitude toward Divine Purpose.

This layered structure lets Morgan preserve continuity from simple life to human spirituality without flattening the difference between amoebic sensitivity, animal intelligence, human thought, and religious consciousness.

⸻

8. Selfhood: not a Cartesian ego, but emergent substantial unity

Morgan’s account of selfhood follows from enjoyment and reference. The “I” is not a simple immaterial substance standing behind experience. It is an emergent synthesis of the organism’s many living and mental items under substantial unity in enjoyment. The self is therefore both embodied and emergent.

In animal life, the self/world distinction is incipient rather than reflective. The organism behaves in an environment, finds centers of interest, feels satisfaction and frustration, and gradually differentiates practical objects from its own bodily and affective life. Selfhood polarizes out of organism-environment transactions; it is not presupposed as a ready-made metaphysical ego.

This makes Morgan’s self closer to a developmental, organismic, and processual self than to a Cartesian subject. The self is real, but its reality is that of a living organized unity with mental enjoyment and reference. It is not a ghostly owner of experiences.

⸻

9. Soul: Morgan’s rejection of animism and his purified use of soul-language

Morgan’s most explicit discussion of “soul” occurs in Life, Mind, and Spirit. He rejects animism when animism means that mental processes belong to an independent entity that controls the body from another realm. Such a view is antithetical to his naturalism. The organism’s mental and physical events belong to one natural realm; the correct distinction is duality of attribute, not dualism of realms.

He then asks what becomes of the soul. The term anima can mean life, mind, or spirit, so Morgan insists that “soul” must be carefully defined. If soul means a dualistic entity separable from and controlling the body, the advocate of concomitance cannot accept it.

Yet Morgan does not simply discard the word. He allows a purified meaning: “soul” may name a level of mental development at which the concept of Spirit enters reflective reference. In that sense, soul is an important stage in the evolution of mind. But it carries no dualistic implication.

This is a major result. For Morgan, soul-language becomes legitimate only after being detoxified from ghostly substance metaphysics. Soul is not a metaphysical tenant inside the body. It is mind at the level where reflective and spiritual reference become possible.

His view of immortality is correspondingly cautious. He does not build the system on discarnate spirits, and he says such spirits may belong to mythology rather than to strict philosophical doctrine. Yet he also retains a religious horizon in which the human person touches the “fringe” of immortality through acknowledgment of God as ultimate substance. Personal survival as a separable soul-substance is not Morgan’s central doctrine; participation in Divine Purpose or Spiritual Substance is closer to his metaphysical center.

⸻

10. Theology is not a distraction: God, Spirit, and Divine Purpose

Morgan’s theology is integral to the philosophy. In the preface to Life, Mind, and Spirit, he quotes approvingly the idea that evolution as the method of creation should be viewed as a revelation of God’s being and working. He rejects a disjunctive antithesis between evolutionary progress and Divine Purpose.

The key theological rule is this: God is not an inserted cause. In Emergent Evolution, Morgan insists that neither life, nor mind, nor reflective consciousness should be explained by divine insertion or intervention at special points. Divine Activity is omnipresent; “God, if in any, is in all.”

This is why Morgan can accept both naturalism and belief in God. Scientific explanation remains naturalistic. Philosophy and religious interpretation may then supplement, not contradict, the scientific account. The same facts may be read naturalistically as evolutionary order and religiously as Divine Purpose.

Morgan’s treatment of “supernatural” is especially revealing. He rejects the idea of two separate orders, natural and supernatural, as though spirit occasionally invades nature from outside. Instead, “supernatural” may be used relatively: life is “supernatural” relative to the inorganic; reflective thought is “supernatural” relative to perception; the religious attitude is “supernatural” relative to ethical or rational life. Each is a higher emergent natural quality and a manifestation of Divine Purpose.

Thus Morgan’s theology is a form of monistic theistic emergentism. Spirit is not merely a late mental property. In Life, Mind, and Spirit, he says life and mind are manifestations of spirit, and spirit is that of which all qualities are manifestations. Later in the work, Divine Purpose includes physical, vital, mental, social, and spiritual advance, and God is discussed as Spiritual Substance and Divine Personality.

The result is neither crude natural theology nor fideistic supernaturalism. Morgan is trying to articulate a world in which the natural order is spiritually interpretable from within. The religious attitude is itself an emergent level of mind: a higher form of reference in which rational order, value, and purpose are acknowledged as Divine Purpose.

⸻

11. Extracting Morgan’s comprehensive philosophy of mind

Morgan’s philosophy of mind can be stated as a set of interconnected theses.

11.1 Mind is a natural aspect of organismic reality

Mind is not outside nature. The organism is one indivisible natural being, analyzable as body and as mind. The physical and psychical are not separable existences; they are distinguishable aspects of one process.

11.2 Consciousness is processual, marginal, and temporally thick

Consciousness is a wave with focal and marginal structure. The present includes traces, tendencies, anticipations, bodily feeling, and subconscious margins. This gives Morgan a non-atomistic psychology and prepares the way for his later theory of enjoyment and reference.

11.3 Mind is embodied in the whole organism, not merely attached to the brain

Morgan gives the brain a central role in developed consciousness, but his mature view treats the whole life-system as the organ of mind. No mind occurs except in concomitance with life-processes, and no life-process occurs without some mental concomitant.

11.4 Psychophysical relation is dual-aspect concomitance, not interaction

Morgan rejects both reductive materialism and dualistic interaction. Physiological process and mental process are not cause and effect across a gulf. They are one concrete occurrence read under different attributes. There is no causal relation between attributes, but one and the same evolutionary process is expressed physically and mentally.

11.5 Emergence is real novelty within continuity

Higher levels depend on lower levels, but their distinctive qualities are not predictable by mere addition. Resultants preserve continuity; emergents introduce new qualitative orders. Life emerges on a physical basis; mind emerges with life; reflective consciousness emerges within mind.

11.6 Mental efficacy is real but non-dualistic

Morgan does not treat consciousness as an epiphenomenal whistle. Mental guidance matters. But this does not mean an immaterial force pushes matter. It means that at the mental level the organized whole has a new pattern of relatedness, and when that relatedness is present, the course of events differs.

11.7 Consciousness is graded: enjoyment, cognitive reference, reflective reference, spiritual reference

Morgan’s mature psychology is not a simple contrast between unconscious matter and conscious humans. It is a hierarchy. Enjoyment is the intrinsic felt side of living process. Cognitive reference appears in practical perception and animal intelligence. Reflective reference appears in conceptual human thought. Spiritual reference appears in the religious attitude toward Divine Purpose.

11.8 Intentionality is “reference,” not physical influence

Mental reference is not a physical causal line between organism and object. Physical influence belongs to the life-story; reference belongs to the mind-story. This lets Morgan preserve intentionality as a distinctive feature of mind without making it a separate substance.

11.9 Selfhood is emergent substantial unity

The self is not a primitive ghost-substance. It emerges as an organized unity of living and mental processes, intrinsically enjoyed and progressively capable of reference to self, world, values, and God.

11.10 Soul is a spiritual-developmental concept, not an animistic entity

The soul, if retained, names mind at the level where Spirit enters reflective reference. It does not name an independent psychic agent controlling the body. Soul-language is acceptable only when purged of dualism.

11.11 Theology completes but does not replace naturalism

Morgan’s God is not a rival scientific cause. Divine Purpose is a philosophical and religious interpretation of the same natural order that science studies. Spirit is the ultimate ground or substance of the process in which life, mind, value, and religious consciousness emerge.

⸻

12. Morgan and the problem of reductionism

Morgan is often misread as a precursor to behaviorism because of the Canon. That is only half true. His methodological caution influenced comparative psychology and later behaviorist practice, but his metaphysics is anti-reductionist. He denies that animal behavior should be explained by needlessly rich mental attributions, yet he also denies that behavior exhausts mind.

Recent English-language scholarship continues to treat the Canon as important for cognitive science. Clatterbuck and Sober describe the Canon as the principle that higher mental faculties should not be postulated when lower ones suffice, and they argue that Morgan had a serious evolutionary argument for the principle rather than a merely anti-anthropomorphic prejudice.

Guido Baggio’s scholarship is useful here because it situates Morgan between biological evolution, psycho-physiology, social development, and pragmatist-functional psychology. Baggio emphasizes Morgan’s commitment to evolution and Jamesian functionalism, while also noting that An Introduction to Comparative Psychology helped shape modern comparative psychology by replacing anecdotal anthropomorphism with a more experimental approach.

Yet Baggio also makes clear that Morgan should not be assimilated to Watsonian behaviorism. Consciousness emerges and later guides behavior; minded behavior has practical meaning; neural processes and consciousness are distinguishable but not separable.

So Morgan’s real position is neither “animals are little humans” nor “animals are mechanisms.” It is that animal mind must be inferred cautiously, developmentally, and comparatively, with attention to levels of mental function.

⸻

13. Multilingual reception and interpretation

The non-English reception does not reveal a wholly separate Morgan, but it sharpens several points that are sometimes flattened in Anglo-American discussions.

French scholarship has been especially helpful in reading Morgan as an anti-reductionist naturalist rather than merely as the author of a parsimony rule. Denis Doat, writing on Dewey and Morgan, presents Morgan’s emergentism as an attempt to hold together continuity and discontinuity: complex phenomena are neither disconnected from physico-chemical nature nor reducible to it. Doat also stresses that Morgan’s emergent evolution is a naturalist, evolutionist, anti-reductionist response to the tension between Darwinian continuity and Bergsonian dualist discontinuity.

The same French discussion is also critical. Doat argues that Morgan retains a progressionist metaphysical structure involving substance, forms, and an almost Aristotelian conception of emergent properties arising from organic totality. On this reading, Morgan’s naturalism is not as fully Darwinian or pragmatist as Dewey’s, because it preserves a teleological and substantialist architecture. This is a fair criticism: Morgan’s “substance” and “Divine Purpose” are not decorative terms. They structure the whole system.

Another French discussion of consciousness notes that Morgan’s strong emergentism may seem surprising if the Canon is read as reductionist. This is exactly the right warning: the Canon is methodological prudence, not a metaphysical denial of consciousness.

Spanish and Latin American materials tend to emphasize the “creative leaps” of Morgan’s emergent evolution: matter, life, reflective consciousness, and increasingly rich levels of reality. One Spanish philosophical presentation describes emergent realities as surprising, non-calculable, and non-predictable from the parts, while distinguishing resultants from emergents. A contrasting Spanish Marxist source criticizes emergent evolution as idealist and bourgeois, and specifically objects to the way Morgan’s account culminates in divinity and treats nature as pervaded by spirit. The criticism is polemical, but it correctly notices that Morgan’s emergentism is not religiously neutral.

Italian scholarship, especially Baggio’s work on instinct and habit, highlights Morgan’s psychogenetic question: how consciousness develops through instinctive activity, habit, and later control. Baggio stresses that consciousness emerges in relation to the first instinctive responses, and that early coordination may occur automatically before consciousness later modifies, perfects, and controls similar activity. This is valuable because it shifts attention from the Canon alone to Morgan’s developmental account of consciousness.

Portuguese-language discussions place Morgan in continuing debates about emergence, the body-mind relation, and consciousness within naturalism. One Portuguese philosophical discussion notes that emergence is invoked precisely for body-mind and consciousness problems in a naturalist viewpoint. Other Portuguese materials show recent translation and discussion of Morgan’s Emergent Evolution and renewed debate over the persistence and overcoming of Morgan’s Canon.

The multilingual picture supports four interpretive conclusions. First, Morgan is widely recognized as anti-reductionist, not merely behaviorist. Second, the Canon is best read as methodological, not eliminativist. Third, Morgan’s theology is often noticed more sharply outside narrow philosophy-of-science treatments. Fourth, his metaphysics remains controversial because it joins naturalism, emergence, substance, hierarchy, and Divine Purpose in one system.

⸻

14. The deepest tensions in Morgan’s philosophy of mind

Morgan’s system is powerful, but it carries real tensions.

The first is the tension between empirical caution and metaphysical breadth. As a comparative psychologist, Morgan is cautious about attributing higher mental processes to animals. As a metaphysician, he speculates about infra-consciousness, psychical correlates, and unrestricted life-mind concomitance. He knows the latter outruns direct evidence, and he presents it as a working hypothesis rather than a demonstrated empirical result.

The second tension concerns mental efficacy. Morgan wants consciousness to guide behavior, but he also denies causal traffic between physical and psychical attributes. His solution is dual-aspect efficacy: the same organized event is effective, and one may describe its guidance psychologically. This avoids Cartesian interaction, but it leaves open the familiar problem of whether mental efficacy is genuinely explanatory or merely a redescription of physical organization.

The third tension is emergence itself. Sometimes Morgan’s unpredictability language sounds epistemic: we cannot predict higher qualities before they appear. Sometimes it sounds ontological: higher qualities are genuinely new features of reality. His strongest position is ontological emergence constrained by natural continuity: the higher is new in kind, but not detached from the lower.

The fourth tension concerns panpsychism or biopsychism. Morgan does not want full-blown panconsciousness, yet he also resists an absolute leap from non-mind to mind. His earlier infra-consciousness and later unrestricted concomitance push toward a broad psychical interpretation of life, and possibly of nature more generally. The safest label is not “panpsychism” but dual-aspect emergentism with a psychical-correlate thesis.

The fifth tension concerns theology and naturalism. Morgan says Divine Purpose supplements rather than interrupts natural explanation. That is coherent if theology is a philosophical-religious interpretation of the whole. It is less persuasive if one demands that God function as an empirically testable cause among causes. Morgan explicitly rejects that demand; his God is not a scientific posit but the spiritual substance or ultimate purpose disclosed under religious acknowledgment.

⸻

15. Final synthetic interpretation

Morgan’s philosophy of mind can be understood as a disciplined attempt to avoid four false alternatives.

Against Cartesian dualism, he says there is no ghostly soul acting on the body. The organism is one substantial unity, analyzable under physical and mental attributes.

Against reductive materialism, he says mind, consciousness, reference, enjoyment, value, and spiritual attitude are real emergent features that cannot be eliminated into lower-level mechanics.

Against behaviorism, he says behavior alone is not the whole story. Behavior gives a body-story, but the mind-story involves enjoyment, reference, consciousness, and guidance.

Against supernatural interventionism, he says God is not inserted at the emergence of life, mind, or reflective consciousness. Divine Activity is omnipresent; the natural order itself is the field of Divine Purpose.

The resulting system is unusually rich. Mind begins as the intrinsic enjoyment of life, becomes practical reference in animal intelligence, becomes reflective self-consciousness in human thought, and becomes spiritual reference in the religious attitude. Soul is not an immaterial thing but the reflective-spiritual deepening of mind. Consciousness is not an accident of mechanism but an emergent mode of organismic reality. Theology is not a foreign addition but the final metaphysical interpretation of the same emergent order.

Morgan’s philosophy of mind can therefore be summarized in one sentence: mind is the emergent, embodied, dual-aspect life of reference and enjoyment, developing from organic sentience through reflective consciousness toward spiritual acknowledgment of Divine Purpose.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
